The Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory is comprised of some 300 technical and scientific personnel engaged in many phases of research directed toward the nuclear propulsion of aircraft.
The nucleus of this effort is now centered on the Aircraft Reactor Experiment - a 3-Mev, high-temperature prototype of a circulating-fuel reactor suitable for the propulsion of aircraft.

This quarterly progress report of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at ORNL records the technical progress of the research on the circulating-fuel reactor and all other ANP research of the Laboratory under its contract, W-7405-eng-26.

Several circulating-fuel aircraft reactor systems employing closely coupled intermediate heat exchangers were described in the last report.(1) The extension of these design studies led to consideration of a spherical-shell type of heat exchanger and shield arrangement that resulted in a saving of about 8000 lb in engineered shield weight over the best arrangements previously examined.(2) In addition, a modified core design having all of the moderator removed from the core and added to the reflector now appears to be an attractive possibility.
With a 12-in.-thick beryllium oxide reflector, critical masses of 23 and 19 lb were obtained for 16- and 32-in.-dia spherical reactor cores.
However, the attenuation length for the fission-producing neutrons from the reflector was about 10 cm, so the high power density region was found to be quite thick.
As a result, the ratio of peak-to-average power density was about 2 for both of the reactors studied.

Because of continued interest in purified NaOH, additional batches have been prepared by the method in which $\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}$ is removed by filtration from a $50\%$ aqueous solution of NaOH and the clear supernatant liquid is dehydrated under vacuum at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The material has assayed $100.0\%$ NaOH, and the $\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}$ content has been less than $0.1\%$ in all runs.

The major effort has again been devoted to the preparation of pure KOH by removing the carbonate as $\mathrm{BaCO_3}$ in an aqueous medium and dehydrating the filtrate under vacuum at $475^{\circ}C$ .

Determination of the solubility of $\mathrm{BaCO_3}$ as a function of KOH concentration indicated that the minimum carbonate content could be obtained if a KOH concentration of 45 to $50\%$ were used.
Under these conditions it appeared possible to prepare a product containing carbonate equivalent to $0.07\%$ $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ .
To date, only one run has given a product corresponding to $0.10\%$ $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ and containing $0.11\%$ Ba.
One run has been made in which pure potassium was reacted with excess water and the resultant solution of KOH was dehydrated by heating under vacuum at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Material from this first run contained $0.10\%$ $\mathbf{K}_2\mathbf{CO}_3$ .

The preparation of this material will be handled in the future by the Y-12 Production Division; a supply of up to 50 lb per week of pure, sublimed $\mathbf{ZrF_4}$ will be available after about June 15.
The preparation of pure liquid fuels of various types has, apparently, been accomplished on a research (5-lb batch) scale.
Installation of similar equipment to turn out 10- to 100-lb batches for larger scale testing should be complete by the time the $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ production permits their use.
Delivery from commercial suppliers was slow, and their products contained up to $20\%$ of $\mathsf{ZrO_2}$ and $\mathsf{ZrOF_2}$ .
The commercial material has been purified satisfactorily by vacuum sublimation in nickel equipment capable of handling 1 to $1.5\mathrm{kg}$ of material per charge.
The sublimers are 4-in. cylinders, 18 inches in length, containing disk-shaped baffles arranged to provide a tortuous path for the vapor.

With the equipment and charge material available at present it appears that the sublimation of 1500-g portions is best conducted at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for about 4 hr at a pressure of 450 microns.
Under these conditions virtually all the $\mathbf{ZrF}_4$ is volatilized and about $80\%$ of the material is deposited on the cold finger.
The sodium and potassium fluorides are hygroscopic and contain up to $0.4\% \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ and about 200 to 300 ppm of sulfur, mostly as sulfate.
The commercial $\mathrm{UF_4}$ contains small amounts of water, up to $1\%$ of hexavalent uranium probably as $\mathrm{UO_2F_2}$ , and traces of $\mathrm{UO_2}$ .
This technique has been adapted to equipment capable of the production of 10- to 100-lb batches (cf., "Fluoride Production" in sec.
Hydrogen is bubbled through the molten liquid for 2 hr at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to reduce the uranium to the quadrivalent state.
Hydrofluorination of the liquid is carried out for at least 1 hr at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or higher to insure conversion of the oxides.
The HF is removed by stripping with an inert gas at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the fuel is forced through a sintered-nickel filter to the receiver.
The major difficulties at present are those associated with maintaining a high-temperature system (600 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) that will contain HF without leaks.
Except for the gas and vacuum lines, the whole system must be maintained at about $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ while transfers are in progress.

Comparison of results in NaF-KF-LiF- $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ fuel and in the corresponding nonuranium-bearing mixture at $800^{\circ}C$ showed that the attack is heavier in the fuel since the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4}$ appears to react with the oxides to form a coating of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{O}_{2}$ .

In both mixtures, vitrified beryllium oxide was found to be the most satisfactory material; it showed weight gains of $7\%$ in the uranium-bearing mixtures and $1\%$ in the uranium-free mixtures but no dimensional changes.

A special recrystallized aluminum oxide, Morganite, satisfactorily resisted attack by the NaF-KF-LiF mixture but gained over $90\%$ when $\mathrm{UF_4}$ was present because of the formation of a heavy layer of $\mathrm{UO_2}$ on the surface.
A similar deposit but a much lower weight gain (18%) occurred when a NaF-BeF $_2$ - $\mathrm{UF_4}$ fuel was used.

Two tests on the dynamic corrosion of liquid metals were performed with the seesaw corrosion apparatus previously described.(1) The tests were made at a $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ hot-zone temperature and a $950^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ cold-zone temperature; the specimens were made of type-310 stainless steel.
One specimen contained lead and the other contained lead plus $3\%$ sodium.
After 187 hr (50,000 cycles) the specimens were sectioned for examination.
Both corrosion and mass transfer were less severe in the test specimen containing $3\%$ sodium.

Seesaw tests with NaF-KF-LiF-UF $_4$ (10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1 mole %) fuel have been made with type-304 and type-304 ELC stainless steel for 190 hr with a hot-zone temperature of $715^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a cold-zone temperature of $575^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The hot zone revealed voids to a depth of 4 mils; whereas a film slightly less than 1 mil thick was observed on the cold-zone surface.
Similar tests with Inconel have repeatedly shown about 5 mils of voids in the hot zone and a small amount of crystal deposition at the cold zone.

The results obtained may be summarized as follows: (1) 3 mole $\%$ potassium metal dissolved at $860^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the molten binary eutectic of KF and NaF; (2) 1.6 mole $\%$ potassium dissolved at $860^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the molten ternary eutectic KF-NaF-LiF; (3) sodium and lithium metal added to these eutectics would reduce considerable amounts of potassium fluoride and some sodium fluoride and thus change the composition and melting points of the
molten mixtures; (4) the solubility of potassium metal at lower temperatures is expected to be considerably smaller, perhaps by a factor of 10 at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and is now being measured.

It appears as a result of thermodynamic considerations that even small concentrations of potassium metal added and dissolved in the fluoride melt, of the order of $0.1\%$ , can be expected to have a considerable beneficial effect on corrosion.

The equilibrium in corrosion reactions of the type $\mathrm{Fe} + 2\mathrm{KF} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{FeF}_2 + 2\mathrm{K}$ may be favorably influenced by the addition of alkali metal to the fluoride mixture.

Thermodynamic estimates of equilibrium constants for reactions of this type, based on the most recent data available in the literature,(9) indicate concentrations of $\mathrm{CrF}_2$ $\mathrm{FeF_2}$ or $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to be of the order of $10^{-4}\cdot 8$ $10^{-5}\cdot 8$ and $10^{-6}\cdot 3$ respectively.
At $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the figures are $10^{-6}$ $10^{-7}$ and $10^{-8}$ respectively, that is, for each metal the concentrations are less by more than a factor
of 10 than at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Thus the constituents of the structural metal dissolved as fluorides at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ can crystallize out as metallic elements at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ as a result of almost complete reversal of the reaction occurring at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Assuming rapid establishment of equilibrium for 2000 g of molten NaF-KF-LiF (11.5-42.0-46.5 mole %) in both legs of the thermal loops and a flow rate of 1 cycle per minute, a quantity of $6000 \times 2000 \times 10^{-58}$ or approximately 15 g of metallic iron would deposit in the cold leg for 6000 cycles in 100 hours.
For chromium and nickel, these quantities are approximately 100 and 6 g, respectively.
These are very crude estimates that are probably correct to only within a factor of 10 except for the relations between Fe, Cr, and Ni, which appear qualitatively of the right order.
The effect of the formation of complexes such as $\mathrm{K}_{3} \mathrm{CrF}_{6}$ , for which thermodynamic data are not available at present, remains to be considered in more accurate calculations.

It is clear that the establishment of equilibrium in a static test, that is, without thermal gradients, would not, with a mole fraction of the order of $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ of $\mathrm{CrF}_2$ , $\mathrm{FeF}_2$ , or $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ , produce any considerable corrosion.

The one $\mathrm{ZrF}_4$ -bearing fluoride measured has a satisfactory viscosity, that is, 8 centipoises at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which decreases to 4 centipoises at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The viscosities of two $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ -bearing mixtures were determined with the Brookfield viscometer in the range 600 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The first mixture contained 51 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ ,
47 mole % NaF, and 2 mole % UF<sub>4</sub> and showed viscosities from 12 centipoises at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ up to about 25 centipoises at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Another preparation of considerably lower $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ concentration contained 12 mole % $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , 76 mole % NaF, and 12 mole % UF<sub>4</sub> and was considerably less viscous at the high-temperature end of the range.
